perturbation.original_text: Within [***] days after the conclusion of each Calendar Quarter to occur during the Term, commencing with the First Commercial Sale of the Product in the Field in the Territory, MMT shall deliver to SIGA a written report containing the following information (each a "Quarterly Report"): [***].
perturbation.changed_text: Within 90 days after the conclusion of each Calendar Quarter to occur during the Term, commencing with the First Commercial Sale of the Product in the Field in the Territory, MMT shall deliver to SIGA a written report containing the following information (each a "Quarterly Report"): [***].
perturbation.explanation: Changing the reporting timeline to 90 days after the end of each quarter introduces a conflict with generally accepted accounting principles (GAAP) and common business practices, which usually require more frequent reporting (e.g., monthly or quarterly) for accurate financial management. While not a direct legal violation, it could violate securities regulations if this delay impairs timely reporting to investors.
perturbation.law_explanation: This regulation requires quarterly reports to be filed by issuers of registered securities, ensuring timely financial disclosures to investors. Setting a 90-day deadline could be construed as hindering the promptness of these disclosures, potentially violating the spirit and intent of securities regulations.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: This page is better viewed with a JavaScript enabled browser. Document not Found Document not found The document you were looking for does not exist. Click here to close this window, or click here to go back.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the reporting timeline from an unspecified number of days to a fixed 90 days. The Law Explanation suggests that this change could hinder the promptness of financial disclosures required by securities regulations. Scraped Law Snippet 1, citing 17 CFR 240.13a-13, mentions 'Quarterly reports on Form 10-Q (§ 249.308a of this chapter),' indicating a regulatory requirement for quarterly reports. Although the snippet doesn't specify a deadline, the Perturbation Explanation indicates that a 90-day delay conflicts with generally accepted accounting principles, raising concerns about timely reporting to investors and compliance with securities regulations. The inconsistency between a potentially faster, legally expected reporting timeline and the explicitly slower 90-day timeline creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party agrees that, during the Term and for a period of [***] years thereafter, such Party and its Affiliates will keep confidential and will not publish or otherwise disclose and will not use for any purpose other than as provided for in this Agreement (which includes the exercise of any rights or the performance of any obligations hereunder) any Confidential Information furnished to it or its Affiliates by the other Party or its Affiliates pursuant to this Agreement, except to the extent expressly authorized by this Agreement or as otherwise agreed to in writing by the Parties; provided, however, that the confidentiality and non-use obligations imposed by this Agreement with respect to trade secrets included in an item of Confidential Information will continue for as long as the disclosing Party continues to treat such Confidential Information as a trade secret.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party agrees that, during the Term and for a period of one (1) year thereafter, such Party and its Affiliates will keep confidential and will not publish or otherwise disclose and will not use for any purpose other than as provided for in this Agreement (which includes the exercise of any rights or the performance of any obligations hereunder) any Confidential Information furnished to it or its Affiliates by the other Party or its Affiliates pursuant to this Agreement, except to the extent expressly authorized by this Agreement or as otherwise agreed to in writing by the Parties; provided, however, that the confidentiality and non-use obligations imposed by this Agreement with respect to trade secrets included in an item of Confidential Information will continue for as long as the disclosing Party continues to treat such Confidential Information as a trade secret.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year after the term's end may be insufficient to protect trade secrets adequately, potentially violating the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) if misappropriation occurs within a longer reasonable timeframe.
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA provides a cause of action for trade secret misappropriation. If the one-year confidentiality clause is deemed unreasonably short to protect legitimate trade secrets, it could be seen as a factor weakening trade secret protection, thus increasing the risk of misappropriation and potential DTSA violations.
perturbation.justification: The modification reduces the confidentiality period to one year, which the perturbation explanation suggests might be insufficient to protect trade secrets under the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA). The law explanation supports this, stating that such a short period could weaken trade secret protection and increase the risk of misappropriation, potentially violating the DTSA. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references the Economic Espionage Act of 1996, which, while not directly the DTSA, indicates a legislative concern with protecting trade secrets. Given these points, the contract change introduces a legal contradiction by potentially failing to adequately protect trade secrets as intended by law.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement becomes effective on the Effective Date and, unless earlier terminated as provided in this ARTICLE 11, shall continue until the five (5) year anniversary of the Effective Date (the "Initial Term"). This Agreement shall be automatically renewed for successive three (3) year terms thereafter (each a "Renewal Term" and together with the Initial Term, the "Term") until and unless (i) either Party provides the other Party written notice of non-renewal no later than ninety (90) days prior the end of the Initial Term or any Renewal Term or (ii) earlier terminated as provided in this ARTICLE 11.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement becomes effective on the Effective Date and, unless earlier terminated as provided in this ARTICLE 11, shall continue until the five (5) year anniversary of the Effective Date (the "Initial Term"). This Agreement shall be automatically renewed for successive three (3) year terms thereafter (each a "Renewal Term" and together with the Initial Term, the "Term") until and unless (i) either Party provides the other Party written notice of non-renewal no later than thirty (30) days prior the end of the Initial Term or any Renewal Term or (ii) earlier terminated as provided in this ARTICLE 11.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the non-renewal notice period to thirty days could be considered unreasonably short in some jurisdictions, particularly if it involves significant business relationships or dependencies. Some state franchise laws, for example, require longer notice periods for non-renewal to protect franchisees.
perturbation.law_explanation: This California statute mandates specific notice periods for franchise agreement terminations and non-renewals. While this specific contract is not necessarily a 'franchise' as defined in this code section, reducing the non-renewal period to 30 days raises the risk of being challenged, particularly if the business relationship exhibits franchise-like characteristics, potentially leading to legal disputes over insufficient notice.

SPECIAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [18400 - 22949.92.2] ( Division 8 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 44. ) CHAPTER 5.5. Franchise Relations [20000 - 20044] ( Chapter 5.5 added by Stats. 1980, Ch. 1355, Sec. 1. ) ARTICLE 4. Nonrenewal [20025 - 20026] ( Article 4 added by Stats. 1980, Ch. 1355, Sec. 1. ) 20025. No franchisor may fail to renew a franchise unless such franchisor provides the franchisee at least 180 days prior written notice of its intention not to renew; and (a) During the 180 days prior to expiration of the franchise the franchisor permits the franchisee to sell his business to a purchaser meeting the franchisor’s then current requirements for granting new franchises, or if the franchisor is not granting a significant number of new franchises, the then current requirements for granting renewal franchises; or (b) (1) The refusal to renew is not for the purpose of converting the franchisee’s business premises to operation by employees or agents of the franchisor for such franchisor’s own account, provided, that nothing in this paragraph shall prohibit a franchisor from exercising a right of first refusal to purchase the franchisee’s business; and (2) Upon expiration of the franchise, the franchisor agrees not to seek t
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the non-renewal notice period from 90 days to 30 days, which contradicts California Code BPC 20025. The law snippet specifies that 'No franchisor may fail to renew a franchise unless such franchisor provides the franchisee at least 180 days prior written notice of its intention not to renew'. Even though the agreement is not explicitly a franchise, the Law Explanation raises the concern that if the business relationship exhibits franchise-like characteristics, the reduced notice period could be challenged, thus creating a legal contradiction.
